related_chapters.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
related_chapters.part_title: A Family of Twelve in Warren County
related_chapters.chapter_title: This One Will Go to College
related_chapters.topic: A Mother's Hope and a We
related_chapters.why: Du Bois frames the Southern university as the goal that lifts a people beyond material striving, and this chapter is a mother singling out one of twelve children for college, the aspiration that carried Terry to Bennett.

related_chapters.subject: Dr. Cleveland Sellers
related_chapters.chapter_title: The University of the Movement
related_chapters.why: The essay treats the college as the place that forms whole people rather than bread-winners, and Sellers reflects on the movement itself as a university, an education in purpose rather than pay.

related_chapters.subject: Lonnie C. King
related_chapters.chapter_title: One Hundred Sixty-Nine Spelman Women
related_chapters.why: Du Bois names Atlanta's college youth as the rising generation, and King recounts the Spelman students of the Atlanta University Center who carried the 1960 student movement.

related_chapters.subject: Freeman A. Hrabowski III
related_chapters.chapter_title: It Does Not Have to Be This Way
related_chapters.why: The essay argues higher learning, not money-getting, is the end of education, and Hrabowski describes the Meyerhoff program he built to produce Black PhDs in science at scale.

related_chapters.subject: John Churchville
related_chapters.chapter_title: A Faulty but Energizing Analysis
related_chapters.topic: Why a Library Became a School
related_chapters.why: Du Bois roots the common school on the university and the search for knowledge, and Churchville explains why his North Philadelphia Freedom Library grew into a school.

related_chapters.subject: Julia Matilda Burns
related_chapters.chapter_title: She Saw What I Could Not See in Myself
related_chapters.why: The essay casts the teacher as the bearer of culture to an untaught people, and Burns credits a tenth-grade English teacher who recognized a capacity she had not seen in herself.

top_voice.subject: Freeman A. Hrabowski III
top_voice.reason: Du Bois calls the universities the wings that bear youth past the temptation of mere gold toward knowledge, and Hrabowski's Meyerhoff Scholars Program producing Black scientists is that argument enacted a century later.

related_chapters.subject: Lonnie C. King
related_chapters.chapter_title: The Evangelist Who Wrote on My Slate
related_chapters.why: Dunbar measures a representative figure by service passed to the next generation, and King traces his own organizing to his grandfather, a rural NAACP recruiter who shaped him early.

related_chapters.subject: Junius Williams
related_chapters.chapter_title: Pop Williams on the Front Porch
related_chapters.why: The essay holds up exemplary elders whose example, not instruction, shapes the young, and Williams describes his father as the role model behind his path.

related_chapters.subject: Dorie Ann Ladner and Joyce Ann Ladner
related_chapters.chapter_title: Grandpa Ben Said We're As Good As Anybody
related_chapters.why: Dunbar locates the source of achievement in inherited self-respect, and the Ladner sisters recall a grandfather who taught them they were as good as anybody, a lesson that carried into SNCC.

related_chapters.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
related_chapters.chapter_title: Wilkins in Elegant Prose, Hooks in the Gospel Style
related_chapters.why: Dunbar's essay is a roll call of representative leaders handing the work forward, and Roxborough reflects on the succession of NAACP leadership from Roy Wilkins to Benjamin Hooks.

related_chapters.subject: Amos C. Brown
related_chapters.chapter_title: Getting to Know Medgar Evers
related_chapters.why: The essay celebrates mentors who lift up the young toward leadership, and Brown recounts being mentored as a teenager by Medgar Evers in the Mississippi NAACP youth work.

related_chapters.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
related_chapters.chapter_title: On Your Mark in Front of Jesse Owens's House
related_chapters.topic: Role Models and a Path to Ministry
related_chapters.why: Dunbar pairs religion and example across generations, and McKinney names the role models who set him toward the ministry, a Morehouse contemporary of King.

top_voice.subject: Lonnie C. King
top_voice.reason: Dunbar defines the representative man as one who achieves for the race and transmits the work onward, and King embodies that line of descent, carrying his NAACP-recruiting grandfather's example into leadership of the Atlanta Student Movement.

related_chapters.subject: Mildred Pitts Walter
related_chapters.chapter_title: Beautiful Because Teacher Told Me So
related_chapters.why: Kealing catalogs self-respect amid abasement as a defining trait, and Walter speaks on natural hair and Black pride, on being taught to see oneself as beautiful.

related_chapters.subject: Euvester Simpson
related_chapters.chapter_title: Walk With Me, Lord
related_chapters.topic: A Washcloth and a Gospel Song
related_chapters.why: Kealing names deep religiousness and endurance under suffering as inborn characteristics, and Simpson recalls a gospel song that carried her through the night in the Winona jail.

related_chapters.subject: Roberta Alexander
related_chapters.part_title: Reflections on a Life in Movements
related_chapters.chapter_title: I Walk Through the World as African American
related_chapters.why: The essay turns on how the race understands its own identity, and Alexander reflects directly on identity and belonging, on walking through the world as African American.

related_chapters.subject: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan
related_chapters.chapter_title: Building a Beloved Community Through Song
related_chapters.why: Kealing singles out the imaginative, musical character of the people and the songs born of hard occasions, and the Carawans describe building community through the freedom-song tradition.

related_chapters.subject: Dorie Ann Ladner and Joyce Ann Ladner
related_chapters.chapter_title: Look White Men in the Eye
related_chapters.why: Kealing weighs self-respect against the deference slavery bred, and the Ladners recount a family lesson to look white men in the eye, a refusal of the inherited posture of submission.

related_chapters.subject: William Saunders
related_chapters.chapter_title: You Are Somebody
related_chapters.why: The essay frames the people's struggle upward as a matter of self-worth, and Saunders recounts the origin of the You Are Somebody message during the Charleston hospital strike.

top_voice.subject: Mildred Pitts Walter
top_voice.reason: Kealing's essay anatomizes the inner character and self-respect of the people, and Walter, who later wrote children's books so the young could see themselves, speaks to that interior sense of worth in her account of Black pride.

related_chapters.subject: Ellie Dahmer
related_chapters.chapter_title: Read That and Tell Me What It Means
related_chapters.topic: Registrar Lynd and the Constitution Test
related_chapters.why: Chesnutt details the understanding clause that let registrars reject Black applicants at will, and Dahmer describes registrar Theron Lynd's constitution-interpretation test in Hattiesburg.

related_chapters.subject: Clarence Magee
related_chapters.chapter_title: The Cat-and-Mouse Test, and the Lawsuit
related_chapters.why: Chesnutt's remedy for disfranchisement runs through the courts, and Magee recounts the cat-and-mouse registration test and the lawsuit brought against it.

related_chapters.subject: Grace Hall Miller
related_chapters.chapter_title: The First Two to Register
related_chapters.why: Chesnutt warns that disfranchisement leaves a people defenseless and exposed to reprisal, and Miller recalls being among the first two to register in Baker County and the retaliation that followed.

related_chapters.subject: Rosie Head
related_chapters.chapter_title: They Never Told Us We Had Passed
related_chapters.why: Chesnutt describes ingenious local machinery built to keep the ballot from Black citizens, and Head recounts being held up and never told she had passed until federal registrars arrived.

related_chapters.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
related_chapters.chapter_title: I Collected Poll Tax in My Beauty Shop
related_chapters.why: Chesnutt itemizes the poll tax among the property qualifications used to bar the vote, and Connor, later a redistricting-suit plaintiff, describes paying and collecting poll tax in her beauty shop.

related_chapters.subject: Timothy Jenkins
related_chapters.chapter_title: A Three-Judge Panel
related_chapters.why: Chesnutt holds that the direct remedy lies through political and legal action, and Jenkins, a SNCC strategist and lawyer, recounts winning the voting cases before a three-judge panel.

top_voice.subject: Timothy Jenkins
top_voice.reason: Chesnutt argues the disfranchised must answer through the courts, Congress, and public opinion, and Jenkins is the SNCC strategist and Howard-trained lawyer who built the legal architecture of voting rights described in his testimony.
